blurb:
Jacquelyn came in from animal control and landed in a foster home where she has been absolutely thriving. She is about two years old, 58 lbs, and the kind of dog who wiggles her way into your lap without being pushy about it. Her foster calls her lovable, huggable, and sweet, and says nothing gets past her on the alert front. She knows what is going on in that house at all times.

She is a full Velcro dog who wants to be wherever you are and gets the most pets in the room. She is playful and silly but also responds well to "sit" and "back" when she gets a little too enthusiastic. She loves the other dogs in her foster home, so if you have a friendly dog at home, she would probably get along great.

One thing worth knowing up front: Jacquelyn has a congenital kidney condition that is easily managed. She needs a few potty breaks during the day, nothing more than a typical dog, and has been great through the night. Her foster says she is doing wonderful. She will likely have a shorter lifespan than some dogs, but she is living fully right now and looking for someone to do that with.

Mostly Mutts is a rescue based in Kennesaw. Head to their website to start the adoption inquiry for Jacquelyn and set up a meet.
shelter_hours: See mostlymutts.org for current hours and appointment info
scoring_notes:
• Congenital condition with a shorter lifespan framing creates the most emotionally compelling 'love fully now' story in the batch
• Thriving-despite-medical-needs narrative builds empathy and reader investment instantly
• Medical complexity will slow adoption; newsletter visibility is genuinely high-impact for this dog

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Ladybug came in from animal control with her siblings, spent some time in a foster home, and has recently moved into the Kitty Aquarium at the Mostly Mutts shelter. She is approximately two months old, about two pounds, and already very clear on what she wants: your attention, right now, please.

She's social in a way that's hard not to smile at. During her photo session she apparently made a strong impression, and in group settings she's been known to stick a paw out just to make sure she's not being overlooked while someone else is getting pets. She gets along great with other cats, loves to play with her siblings, and is already litter trained. She'll need you to finish out her kitten shot series as she grows, and her spay is required and will be scheduled once she's big enough.

If you have another cat at home who would enjoy a small, enthusiastic new roommate, Ladybug is worth a visit. She's at the Mostly Mutts shelter in Kennesaw. Call or email to find out how to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Two-month-old kitten with outsized personality is the highest impulse-adoption candidate in the group
• The paw-reaching-out-for-attention visual is newsletter gold and will drive clicks and shares
• Recently arrived from animal control — low shelter time limits urgency, but cuteness compensates

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Gina has been through a lot for an 11-month-old. She came into Mostly Mutts as part of the Go-Gos litter, got adopted, and then came back when she started having some stomach trouble. Several months of vet care and food changes later, she's doing better, though she does still vomit occasionally and nobody's quite figured out why yet. She's not a mystery in any other way though. Her foster calls her the best cat ever, and that tracks.

She's playful and sweet, loves to snuggle, and carries herself like a cat who still has no idea she's almost a year old. She's petite (about 5 lbs) and will probably stay that way, which means if you've ever wanted a cat with permanent kitten energy, Gina is a pretty good candidate. She gets along well with other cats and has been unbothered by a leashed dog. She also likes the kids she's met in her foster home, as long as they're gentle.

The one practical thing to know: because she's on a special diet, a home where she can eat separately from other pets would make life easier for everyone. It's manageable, just worth planning for. She's at Mostly Mutts in Kennesaw and is ready to meet you. Call or email to get the process started.
scoring_notes:
• Returned-once kitten with a medical mystery adds emotional depth that resonates with readers
• Foster's 'best cat ever' endorsement plus permanent kitten energy is a highly marketable hook
• Months of vet care since return signals meaningful time waiting for her second chance

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Iceman was adopted from Mostly Mutts as a kitten, grew up in a family that loved him, and then life changed and he ended up back at the rescue. He's five years old now, fully settled into who he is, and just looking for a home that sticks.

He's a lap-and-hangout cat. His people are his favorite thing, and he's happy to sit with you and watch the world go by. He's litter trained, neutered, microchipped, up to date on vaccines, and tested combo negative. At 11 lbs he's a solid, handsome guy. His previous family noted he wasn't into other male cats, but he's done well with female cats and has been great with kids in past foster situations. If you've got a laid-back girl cat at home who could use a companion, he's worth a meet and greet.

His adoption fee is $175 and he's currently in a foster home in Kennesaw. Mostly Mutts can help you set up a time to meet him. Reach out by phone or email to get started.
scoring_notes:
• Adopted-then-returned arc at age five carries real emotional urgency for readers seeking connection
• Low-maintenance lap cat profile makes him one of the most straightforward adoption pitches in the group
• Grew up loved and came back through no fault of his own — a built-in narrative readers will rally around

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Iceman was adopted from Mostly Mutts as a kitten, had a good five years with his family, and then his family's situation changed. So here he is, back at the rescue at age five, fully formed as a cat and ready for whoever is lucky enough to adopt him next.

He's 11 pounds, vaccinated, neutered, microchipped, and combo tested negative. According to his listing, he likes hanging out with his people and watching the world go by. He's litter trained, keeps his space clean, and his previous foster home had four kids who he loved. He's a calm, easy cat who just needs somewhere to land.

One thing worth knowing: his previous owner mentioned he wasn't a fan of other male cats. A female cat roommate would probably be fine, and the rescue is open to meet and greets to see how it goes. Dogs are unknown since he hasn't been around them recently, but given how easygoing he sounds, it's worth asking.

Iceman is in a foster home in Kennesaw through Mostly Mutts. His adoption fee is $175. Email or call to set up a visit.
scoring_notes:
• Fully vetted, litter trained, and proven with four kids makes him one of the most plug-and-play adoption candidates in this batch
• Five years of family life cut short by circumstance is a quietly emotional hook that will resonate with readers who have experienced life changes
• Returned as an adult after a stable multi-year home implies a meaningful wait ahead without intervention from newsletter readers

blurb:
Yasmeen is eight years old and has clearly figured out what matters in life: people she can trust, a home she feels safe in, and a family she can love hard. She came to Mostly Mutts from animal control, went through their prison training program, and has been in a foster home ever since. Her foster mom says her future adopters are going to hit the jackpot.

She takes a little time to warm up to new people, which is fair given where she came from. But her track record in the training program shows that once she decides you are her person, it happens fast. In her foster home she is calm, loving, and good with both dogs and cats. She does get protective when strangers come into her space, so the recommended move is to introduce new people outside the home first. That is a manageable ask, and it is a habit worth building with any dog.

She would do best with dog-savvy kids 15 and up. If you have younger kids and want to explore it, a meet with her trainer is the right first step.

Yasmeen is ready and waiting in her foster home. Start the adoption inquiry through the Mostly Mutts website, and if there are kids or other pets in your household, they can help set up the right kind of introduction.
shelter_hours: See mostlymutts.org for current hours and appointment info
scoring_notes:
• Eight-year-old senior from animal control who completed prison training is the most layered and redemptive story in the group
• Foster's 'jackpot' declaration paired with her loyalty-once-bonded profile is a powerful emotional pitch for the right reader
• Age, household restrictions, and slow warm-up mean she likely waits longest — newsletter exposure is highest-stakes here

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
Two months old, two pounds, and already completely sure of herself. Ladybug came into Mostly Mutts from animal control with her siblings, spent time in a foster home, and recently moved to the Kitty Aquarium at the shelter while she waits for the right person to walk through the door.

Her foster noticed early on that Ladybug has a thing about being included. When someone was petting one of the other kittens, she'd reach a paw out just to make sure she wasn't forgotten. That's not neediness, that's just personality, and she's got plenty of it. She's social, litter trained, and good with other cats.

She's had her first round of kitten shots but will need you to finish those as she grows. She's microchipped, and her spay will be scheduled once she's big enough. She came from animal control so her history with dogs isn't known, but she grew up around other cats and handled it great.

Ladybug is now at the Mostly Mutts shelter location. Reach out by phone or email to set up a time to meet her.
scoring_notes:
• Two-month-old kitten with a vivid personality and no serious flags is about as easy a sell as a shelter animal gets
• The paw-reaching-out detail is immediately shareable and gives newsletter readers a visual, emotional moment to latch onto
• Recently moved from foster to shelter floor suggests she is early in her wait, limiting urgency but increasing visibility opportunity

blurb:
Yasmeen's foster mom says adopting her would be hitting the jackpot. After spending time with her, it's not hard to see why. She's eight years old, about 58 pounds, and the kind of dog who just wants to love her people and be loved back. She went through a prison foster training program, learned a lot, and is now settled into a foster home waiting for her person to show up.

She took about a day to fully trust her foster, and once that trust was there, she opened right up. She's good with cats (both male and female, per her foster), and she warms up to other dogs once the initial meeting is out of the way. She does have some protective instincts around strangers coming into the home, which her trainers have worked on. The recommendation is to introduce new people outside the house first, which is a pretty simple adjustment and one that's made a real difference for her.

She'd do best with dog-savvy kids 15 and up, and any introductions with other dogs should be done as a proper meet and greet. If you're looking for a loyal, loving dog who's going to take her job as your companion seriously, Yasmeen is worth the trip.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Submit an inquiry on their website to set up a meeting, and they'll likely want her trainer involved for introductions.
scoring_notes:
• Prison training, cat compatibility, and a foster calling her a jackpot give seasoned adopters strong, credible reasons to inquire immediately
• The one-day trust window followed by full emotional openness is a nuanced, touching detail that elevates her above a standard senior dog profile
• Eight years old with protective instincts requiring structured introductions means she faces real adoption headwinds and genuinely needs newsletter visibility to find her person

shelter_name: Mostly Mutts Animal Rescue & Adoption, Inc.
blurb:
She came in as part of a litter the rescue named after the Go-Gos, got adopted, and then life threw her a curveball. Gina was returned after about a month when she started having some stomach issues. Several months of vet care and food changes later, she's doing better, though she does still vomit occasionally and nobody has figured out exactly why. Her foster calls her the best cat ever anyway, and honestly, reading her profile, it's hard to argue.

Gina is about 11 months old and only five pounds, and she's probably going to stay right around there. She's independent but also loves to snuggle, which is kind of the ideal cat personality. She's playful, sweet, and gets along well with the two adult cats in her foster home.

The one thing to plan for is her diet. She's on a special food, so if you have other cats at home, you'd need a feeding setup that keeps her meals separate. It's totally doable with a little planning. She's also met a leashed dog without flinching, and she's been great with the kids in her foster home. She just needs someone patient and willing to work with her quirk.

Gina is located in the Kennesaw area through Mostly Mutts. You can reach out by email or phone to get the process started and set up a meeting.
scoring_notes:
• Returned after adoption with ongoing mystery health issue adds friction but foster's 'best cat ever' endorsement is a strong trust signal
• Go-Gos litter origin plus return-and-recovery arc creates a genuinely compelling underdog narrative for readers
• Several months of vet care post-return suggests extended time in rescue system, building urgency for the right adopter to step up

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a little nubby tail, and more energy than her last family knew what to do with. She is six years old, about 35 lbs, and a hound mix who needs someone who actually wants to go do things. Hikes, long walks, fetch in the yard, whatever it is, she is in. Then she will come home and curl up next to you on the couch. Her foster puts her energy level at a 7 or 8, but says the cuddle level matches it exactly.

She went through the Mostly Mutts prison training program and came out knowing a lot. She is sweet and affectionate with people and loves spending time with her humans. She does some gentle mouthy play when she gets excited, never aggressive, just enthusiastic. Older kids, maybe 10 and up, would probably be a great fit for that reason.

Josie does best as the only dog and should skip homes with cats. That is a real consideration, but if you have an active household without other pets, she fits that picture pretty well.

She is currently in a foster home through Mostly Mutts. Submit an adoption inquiry on their website to get the process started and go meet her.
shelter_hours: See mostlymutts.org for current hours and appointment info
scoring_notes:
• Prison training program credential is a distinctive, trust-building detail that elevates her above typical shelter dogs
• Returned by a family that couldn't match her energy — a relatable story that speaks directly to active-lifestyle readers
• Only-dog requirement narrows her pool meaningfully, making newsletter reach genuinely useful for finding the right fit

blurb:
Jacquelyn came into rescue from animal control, landed in a foster home with three other dogs, and has been thriving ever since. She's about two years old, around 58 pounds, and her foster describes her as lovable, huggable, and genuinely fun to be around. She's the kind of dog who wants to be right in the middle of whatever you're doing, not because she's pushy, but because she just really likes her people.

She's playful and silly and apparently an excellent alert dog (her foster jokes that nothing gets by her). She's learned sit, back, and stop when asked, and she loves playing with the dogs in her foster home. She does best without cats, and if you have toddlers, she's probably not the right fit since her enthusiasm can get a little bumpy. Older kids are worth a meet and greet.

One thing worth knowing: Jacquelyn has a congenital kidney defect. It's manageable and hasn't slowed her down at all. She does well with a few outdoor breaks during the day and sleeps through the night without issue. She'd do best with someone who works from home or has a flexible schedule. She's also best suited to a home with another dog who can match her energy.

Mostly Mutts is based in Kennesaw. You can inquire about Jacquelyn through their website and set up a meet and greet from there.
scoring_notes:
• Manageable congenital kidney condition narrows the pool but work-from-home culture makes her WFH requirement increasingly matchable
• A dog thriving despite a health diagnosis with a foster who clearly adores her is a resilience story that drives reader empathy and action
• Animal control origin plus medical complexity likely extends her timeline, making newsletter exposure meaningfully higher-stakes for her outcome

blurb:
Josie has floppy ears, a little nubby tail, and enough energy to keep up with just about anyone who likes to actually do things. She's a six-year-old hound mix, about 35 pounds, and she's been through the Mostly Mutts prison training program, a short adoption that didn't work out (energy mismatch, not a behavior problem), and is now in a foster home ready to try again with the right family.

Her foster puts her energy at a seven or eight out of ten, which is honest. She loves to play, loves people, and loves to cuddle once the fun is done. She does some gentle mouthy play that's never aggressive, just enthusiastic. She settled in during her shelter bath after a slow start and was giving kisses by the end of it. That's pretty much Josie in a nutshell.

She needs to be the only dog in the home and does best without cats. Kids ten and up are probably a good fit, and it's worth doing a meet and greet to confirm. If you like to hike, run, or just move around a lot and want a dog who will match that and then crash on the couch with you afterward, Josie is worth a visit.

Mostly Mutts is in Kennesaw. Head to their website to submit an inquiry and get a meeting set up.
scoring_notes:
• Prison training program plus a failed adoption for non-behavioral reasons positions her as a proven, misunderstood dog ready for the right active home
• The bath scene arc from reluctant to giving kisses is a tight, vivid story beat that newsletter readers will remember and share
• A six-year-old hound who has cycled through prison program, an adoption, and a return carries one of the longer implied timelines in this group, raising urgency significantly
